Real Analysis

Uniform Continuity

Uniform Continuity

Intuition: “One Size Fits All”

The Min-Max and Boundedness Theorems of the previous lecture showed that continuity on a closed, bounded interval has remarkably strong consequences. This lecture identifies another such consequence — but first, we need a strengthened notion of continuity itself, since ordinary continuity is not quite enough on its own to capture what makes closed, bounded intervals special.

Recall that ordinary continuity means a small change in the input xxx produces a small change in the output f(x)f(x)f(x). The catch is that “small” can mean something different depending on where on the graph you are standing. Uniform continuity insists that the same notion of “small” works everywhere at once.

The “Steering Wheel” Analogy

Imagine driving along a winding road, trying to stay within your lane (the lane width is your allowed error ϵ\epsilonϵ).

  • Ordinary continuity: you can always stay in the lane, but how much you’re allowed to wiggle the wheel (your δ\deltaδ) depends on where you are. On a straight stretch, an inch of wiggle is fine; on a hairpin turn, even a millimeter sends you off the road.
  • Uniform continuity: the road is predictable enough that one safe wiggle room works for the entire trip — whatever margin keeps you safe on the worst turn keeps you safe everywhere.
The Geometric Intuition: The Sliding Box

Picture a small transparent box, 2ϵ2\epsilon2ϵ tall and 2δ2\delta2δ wide, centered on a point of the graph.

  • If fff is merely continuous, then for each point you can shrink the box’s width until the graph exits only through the sides — but the required width may shrink as you move to different points.
  • If fff is uniformly continuous, one fixed box size works no matter where you slide it along the entire curve.

A function that gets infinitely steep somewhere defeats any single box: as the graph steepens, the box must be narrowed further and further to keep the curve from poking out the top or bottom, so no single width ever works.

Uniformly Continuous vs. Not
  • f(x)=2xf(x) = 2xf(x)=2x: a straight line with constant slope. Keeping f(x)f(x)f(x) within 0.10.10.1 only ever requires keeping xxx within 0.050.050.05 — this works identically at x=0x=0x=0, x=100x=100x=100, or x=1,000,000x=1{,}000{,}000x=1,000,000.
  • f(x)=sin⁡xf(x) = \sin xf(x)=sinx: oscillates forever, but its slope never exceeds 111 in magnitude — its steepness is capped, so a single sliding box works for the whole infinite wave.
  • f(x)=x2f(x) = x^2f(x)=x2 on all of R\mathbb{R}R: flat near 000, but nearly vertical for large xxx. A wiggle room that works at x=1x=1x=1 fails catastrophically at x=1,000,000x=1{,}000{,}000x=1,000,000 — no single δ\deltaδ works everywhere.
  • f(x)=1/xf(x) = 1/xf(x)=1/x for x>0x>0x>0: as x→0+x \to 0^+x→0+, the graph becomes infinitely steep. However small the sliding box, sliding it close enough to 000 eventually pokes the curve out the top and bottom.
Summary

Uniform continuity means a function is “well-behaved” everywhere at once — no sudden, infinitely steep drops or unpredictable spikes that force constant readjustment of the error margin.

Ordinary vs. Uniform Continuity

We now make this precise, contrasting the two definitions side by side to isolate exactly where they differ.

Continuity at a Point

A function f:A→Rf: A \to \mathbb{R}f:A→R is continuous at a point a∈Aa \in Aa∈A if

∀ ϵ>0, ∃ δ>0 such that ∀ x∈A, ∣x−a∣<δ  ⟹  ∣f(x)−f(a)∣<ϵ.\forall\, \epsilon>0,\ \exists\, \delta>0 \text{ such that } \forall\, x\in A,\ |x-a|<\delta \implies |f(x)-f(a)|<\epsilon.∀ϵ>0, ∃δ>0 such that ∀x∈A, ∣x−a∣<δ⟹∣f(x)−f(a)∣<ϵ.

Here δ\deltaδ may depend on both ϵ\epsilonϵ and the point aaa — often written δ=δ(ϵ,a)\delta = \delta(\epsilon,a)δ=δ(ϵ,a).

Uniform Continuity

A function f:A→Rf: A \to \mathbb{R}f:A→R is uniformly continuous on AAA if

∀ ϵ>0, ∃ δ(ϵ)>0 such that ∀ x,u∈A, ∣x−u∣<δ(ϵ)  ⟹  ∣f(x)−f(u)∣<ϵ.\forall\, \epsilon>0,\ \exists\, \delta(\epsilon)>0 \text{ such that } \forall\, x,u\in A,\ |x-u|<\delta(\epsilon) \implies |f(x)-f(u)|<\epsilon.∀ϵ>0, ∃δ(ϵ)>0 such that ∀x,u∈A, ∣x−u∣<δ(ϵ)⟹∣f(x)−f(u)∣<ϵ.
The Key Difference

In uniform continuity, δ\deltaδ depends only on ϵ\epsilonϵ — never on the specific points x,u∈Ax,u \in Ax,u∈A. This is a genuinely stronger requirement: uniform continuity on AAA implies continuity at every point of AAA, but the converse can fail, as the examples below show.

Examples of Uniform Continuity

f(x)=2xf(x) = 2xf(x)=2x on (0,∞)(0,\infty)(0,∞)

Proof

For any x,u∈(0,∞)x,u \in (0,\infty)x,u∈(0,∞),

∣f(x)−f(u)∣=∣2x−2u∣=2∣x−u∣.|f(x)-f(u)| = |2x-2u| = 2|x-u|.∣f(x)−f(u)∣=∣2x−2u∣=2∣x−u∣.

To guarantee ∣f(x)−f(u)∣<ϵ|f(x)-f(u)| < \epsilon∣f(x)−f(u)∣<ϵ, it suffices that ∣x−u∣<ϵ/2|x-u| < \epsilon/2∣x−u∣<ϵ/2. So take

δ(ϵ)=ϵ2.\delta(\epsilon) = \frac{\epsilon}{2}.δ(ϵ)=2ϵ​.

This single choice of δ\deltaδ works identically for every pair x,ux,ux,u, since the constant 222 bounding the difference does not depend on where x,ux,ux,u lie — the function changes at a constant rate everywhere.

f(x)=1/xf(x) = 1/xf(x)=1/x on (a,∞)(a,\infty)(a,∞) for a>0a>0a>0

This example uses the same conjugate-style factoring seen in the reciprocal limit proof of Lecture 13, but now the bound must hold uniformly across the whole restricted domain rather than just near one point.

Proof

For any x,u∈(a,∞)x,u \in (a,\infty)x,u∈(a,∞),

∣f(x)−f(u)∣=∣1x−1u∣=∣x−u∣∣xu∣.|f(x)-f(u)| = \left|\frac1x-\frac1u\right| = \frac{|x-u|}{|xu|}.∣f(x)−f(u)∣=​x1​−u1​​=∣xu∣∣x−u∣​.

Since x,u>ax,u > ax,u>a, we have xu>a2xu > a^2xu>a2, so

∣f(x)−f(u)∣=∣x−u∣∣xu∣<1a2∣x−u∣.|f(x)-f(u)| = \frac{|x-u|}{|xu|} < \frac{1}{a^2}|x-u|.∣f(x)−f(u)∣=∣xu∣∣x−u∣​<a21​∣x−u∣.

To guarantee ∣f(x)−f(u)∣<ϵ|f(x)-f(u)|<\epsilon∣f(x)−f(u)∣<ϵ, it suffices that ∣x−u∣<a2ϵ|x-u| < a^2\epsilon∣x−u∣<a2ϵ. So take

δ(ϵ)=a2ϵ.\delta(\epsilon) = a^2\epsilon.δ(ϵ)=a2ϵ.

This works uniformly because, on (a,∞)(a,\infty)(a,∞), the inputs never approach 000 — the factor 1/(xu)1/(xu)1/(xu) is bounded above by the fixed constant 1/a21/a^21/a2, independent of the specific x,ux,ux,u chosen.

Nonuniform Continuity

The Nonuniform Continuity Criterion

Just as with sequential convergence (Lecture 9) and non-Cauchyness (Lecture 12), it is useful to have a sequential negation of uniform continuity — one that produces two sequences whose inputs merge together while their outputs stay apart.

Nonuniform Continuity Criterion

The following are equivalent:

  1. fff is not uniformly continuous on AAA.
  2. There exists ϵ0>0\epsilon_0 > 0ϵ0​>0 such that for every δ>0\delta > 0δ>0, there exist xδ,uδ∈Ax_\delta, u_\delta \in Axδ​,uδ​∈A with
∣xδ−uδ∣<δand∣f(xδ)−f(uδ)∣≥ϵ0.|x_\delta - u_\delta| < \delta \quad\text{and}\quad |f(x_\delta)-f(u_\delta)| \ge \epsilon_0.∣xδ​−uδ​∣<δand∣f(xδ​)−f(uδ​)∣≥ϵ0​.
  1. There exists ϵ0>0\epsilon_0 > 0ϵ0​>0 and two sequences (xn),(un)⊆A(x_n), (u_n) \subseteq A(xn​),(un​)⊆A such that
lim⁡n→∞∣xn−un∣=0and∣f(xn)−f(un)∣≥ϵ0 for all n.\lim_{n\to\infty}|x_n - u_n| = 0 \quad\text{and}\quad |f(x_n)-f(u_n)| \ge \epsilon_0 \text{ for all } n.n→∞lim​∣xn​−un​∣=0and∣f(xn​)−f(un​)∣≥ϵ0​ for all n.

Example: f(x)=1/xf(x) = 1/xf(x)=1/x on (0,∞)(0,\infty)(0,∞) Is Not Uniformly Continuous

Contrast this with the restricted domain (a,∞)(a,\infty)(a,∞) above, where 1/x1/x1/x was uniformly continuous — the difference is entirely due to the behavior near 000, which the restriction to (a,∞)(a,\infty)(a,∞) excluded.

Proof

Let ϵ0=1\epsilon_0 = 1ϵ0​=1, and define

xn=1n,un=1n+1.x_n = \frac1n, \qquad u_n = \frac{1}{n+1}.xn​=n1​,un​=n+11​.

Both sequences lie in (0,∞)(0,\infty)(0,∞), and their distance vanishes:

∣xn−un∣=∣1n−1n+1∣=1n(n+1)→0.|x_n - u_n| = \left|\frac1n - \frac{1}{n+1}\right| = \frac{1}{n(n+1)} \to 0.∣xn​−un​∣=​n1​−n+11​​=n(n+1)1​→0.

But the outputs stay exactly 111 apart:

∣f(xn)−f(un)∣=∣n−(n+1)∣=1.|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| = |n - (n+1)| = 1.∣f(xn​)−f(un​)∣=∣n−(n+1)∣=1.

By condition (3) of the Nonuniform Continuity Criterion, f(x)=1/xf(x)=1/xf(x)=1/x is not uniformly continuous on (0,∞)(0,\infty)(0,∞).

The Uniform Continuity Theorem

We now arrive at the main result: on a closed, bounded interval, continuity — a purely local, pointwise condition — automatically upgrades to uniform continuity. The proof follows precisely the same three-step template as the Boundedness and Min-Max Theorems from the previous lecture: build a sequence witnessing the failure, extract a convergent subsequence via Bolzano–Weierstrass, then use sequential continuity to derive a contradiction.

Uniform Continuity Theorem

Let III be a closed and bounded interval. If f:I→Rf: I \to \mathbb{R}f:I→R is continuous on III, then fff is uniformly continuous on III.

Proof by Contradiction

Suppose fff is continuous on III but not uniformly continuous. By the Nonuniform Continuity Criterion, there exists ϵ0>0\epsilon_0 > 0ϵ0​>0 and sequences (xn),(un)⊂I(x_n),(u_n) \subset I(xn​),(un​)⊂I with

lim⁡n→∞∣xn−un∣=0and∣f(xn)−f(un)∣≥ϵ0 for all n.\lim_{n\to\infty}|x_n-u_n| = 0 \quad\text{and}\quad |f(x_n)-f(u_n)| \ge \epsilon_0 \text{ for all } n.n→∞lim​∣xn​−un​∣=0and∣f(xn​)−f(un​)∣≥ϵ0​ for all n.

Since (xn)⊂I(x_n) \subset I(xn​)⊂I and III is bounded, the Bolzano–Weierstrass Theorem (Lecture 10) gives a convergent subsequence (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​), say xnk→x∗x_{n_k} \to x^*xnk​​→x∗. Since III is closed, x∗∈Ix^* \in Ix∗∈I.

The corresponding uuu-subsequence shares the same limit. Since ∣xn−un∣→0|x_n - u_n| \to 0∣xn​−un​∣→0, the subsequence ∣xnk−unk∣→0|x_{n_k}-u_{n_k}| \to 0∣xnk​​−unk​​∣→0 as well (Lecture 9), so

∣unk−x∗∣≤∣unk−xnk∣+∣xnk−x∗∣→0|u_{n_k} - x^*| \le |u_{n_k}-x_{n_k}| + |x_{n_k}-x^*| \to 0∣unk​​−x∗∣≤∣unk​​−xnk​​∣+∣xnk​​−x∗∣→0

by the triangle inequality, giving unk→x∗u_{n_k} \to x^*unk​​→x∗ too.

Continuity forces the outputs together. Since fff is continuous at x∗∈Ix^* \in Ix∗∈I, the sequential criterion for continuity gives

lim⁡k→∞f(xnk)=f(x∗),lim⁡k→∞f(unk)=f(x∗).\lim_{k\to\infty} f(x_{n_k}) = f(x^*), \qquad \lim_{k\to\infty} f(u_{n_k}) = f(x^*).k→∞lim​f(xnk​​)=f(x∗),k→∞lim​f(unk​​)=f(x∗).

By the Subtraction Limit Law (Lecture 7),

lim⁡k→∞∣f(xnk)−f(unk)∣=∣f(x∗)−f(x∗)∣=0.\lim_{k\to\infty} |f(x_{n_k})-f(u_{n_k})| = |f(x^*)-f(x^*)| = 0.k→∞lim​∣f(xnk​​)−f(unk​​)∣=∣f(x∗)−f(x∗)∣=0.

But this contradicts ∣f(xn)−f(un)∣≥ϵ0>0|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| \ge \epsilon_0 > 0∣f(xn​)−f(un​)∣≥ϵ0​>0 for every nnn, which forces the same bound along the subsequence. Hence fff must be uniformly continuous on III.

Same proof skeleton, new conclusion

Compare this to the Boundedness Theorem (Lecture 15): both extract a bounded sequence, pass to a convergent subsequence via Bolzano–Weierstrass, land inside III using closedness, and then invoke sequential continuity to force a contradiction. The only new ingredient here is tracking two sequences whose distance vanishes, so that both subsequences converge to the same limit point.

Lipschitz Continuity

The Uniform Continuity Theorem guarantees uniform continuity exists on closed, bounded intervals, but gives no explicit δ(ϵ)\delta(\epsilon)δ(ϵ). The Lipschitz condition below is a simple sufficient criterion that, when it holds, makes constructing δ(ϵ)\delta(\epsilon)δ(ϵ) immediate — and it holds on any domain, not just closed, bounded ones (compare Example 1 above, 2x2x2x on all of (0,∞)(0,\infty)(0,∞)).

Lipschitz Function

Let A⊆RA \subseteq \mathbb{R}A⊆R and f:A→Rf: A \to \mathbb{R}f:A→R. Then fff is Lipschitz on AAA if there exists a constant k>0k > 0k>0 such that

∀ x,y∈A,∣f(x)−f(y)∣≤k∣x−y∣.\forall\, x,y \in A,\quad |f(x)-f(y)| \le k|x-y|.∀x,y∈A,∣f(x)−f(y)∣≤k∣x−y∣.
Lipschitz Implies Uniformly Continuous

If fff is Lipschitz on AAA, then fff is uniformly continuous on AAA.

Proof

Given ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0, choose δ(ϵ)=ϵ/k\delta(\epsilon) = \epsilon/kδ(ϵ)=ϵ/k. Then for any x,u∈Ax,u \in Ax,u∈A with ∣x−u∣<δ(ϵ)|x-u| < \delta(\epsilon)∣x−u∣<δ(ϵ),

∣f(x)−f(u)∣≤k∣x−u∣<k⋅ϵk=ϵ.|f(x)-f(u)| \le k|x-u| < k\cdot\frac{\epsilon}{k} = \epsilon.∣f(x)−f(u)∣≤k∣x−u∣<k⋅kϵ​=ϵ.

Hence fff is uniformly continuous on AAA.

Recognizing the pattern

This is exactly the same style of argument used in Examples 1 and 2 above (2x2x2x and 1/x1/x1/x): both proofs first bounded ∣f(x)−f(u)∣|f(x)-f(u)|∣f(x)−f(u)∣ by a constant times ∣x−u∣|x-u|∣x−u∣, then solved for δ\deltaδ. The Lipschitz theorem simply packages that recurring technique into a single reusable criterion.

Connection to the Mean Value Theorem

The Lipschitz condition can feel like it needs to be verified case by case, as in the examples above — but a bounded derivative gives an automatic, mechanical way to establish it, via the Mean Value Theorem (MVT).

Recall the MVT: if fff is differentiable, then for any a,ba,ba,b there exists ccc between them with

f(a)−f(b)=f′(c)(a−b).f(a) - f(b) = f'(c)(a-b).f(a)−f(b)=f′(c)(a−b).

Rearranging identifies f′(c)f'(c)f′(c) with the secant slope between aaa and bbb:

f(a)−f(b)a−b=f′(c).\frac{f(a)-f(b)}{a-b} = f'(c).a−bf(a)−f(b)​=f′(c).

Taking absolute values, for any x,y∈Ax,y \in Ax,y∈A there exists θ\thetaθ between xxx and yyy with

∣f(x)−f(y)∣∣x−y∣=∣f′(θ)∣.\frac{|f(x)-f(y)|}{|x-y|} = |f'(\theta)|.∣x−y∣∣f(x)−f(y)∣​=∣f′(θ)∣.
Bounded Derivative Implies Lipschitz

If ∣f′(x)∣≤k|f'(x)| \le k∣f′(x)∣≤k for all x∈Ax \in Ax∈A, then fff is Lipschitz on AAA with constant kkk, and hence uniformly continuous on AAA.

Why this connects back to the sliding-box intuition

A bounded derivative is precisely the algebraic version of “the slope is capped,” which was exactly the informal criterion used earlier to explain why sin⁡x\sin xsinx is uniformly continuous while x2x^2x2 on all of R\mathbb{R}R is not. The MVT makes this intuition rigorous: a global bound on ∣f′∣|f'|∣f′∣ translates directly into a global Lipschitz constant.

Examples of Lipschitz Functions

f(x)=2xf(x) = 2xf(x)=2x on R\mathbb{R}R

Example

Since f′(x)=2f'(x) = 2f′(x)=2 everywhere, fff is Lipschitz on all of R\mathbb{R}R with k=2k=2k=2 — consistent with the direct computation in Example 1 above, now obtained instead from the derivative bound.

f(x)=x2f(x) = x^2f(x)=x2 on a Bounded Interval [a,b][a,b][a,b]

Unlike 2x2x2x, this function is not Lipschitz on all of R\mathbb{R}R, since its derivative 2x2x2x is unbounded — matching the earlier observation that x2x^2x2 fails to be uniformly continuous on all of R\mathbb{R}R. Restricting to a bounded interval fixes this.

Proof

For x,u∈[a,b]x,u \in [a,b]x,u∈[a,b],

∣x2−u2∣=∣x+u∣ ∣x−u∣.|x^2-u^2| = |x+u|\,|x-u|.∣x2−u2∣=∣x+u∣∣x−u∣.

By the triangle inequality, ∣x+u∣≤∣x∣+∣u∣|x+u| \le |x|+|u|∣x+u∣≤∣x∣+∣u∣. Let M=max⁡{∣a∣,∣b∣}M = \max\{|a|,|b|\}M=max{∣a∣,∣b∣}; since x,u∈[a,b]x,u \in [a,b]x,u∈[a,b], ∣x∣,∣u∣≤M|x|,|u| \le M∣x∣,∣u∣≤M. Hence

∣x2−u2∣≤(∣x∣+∣u∣)∣x−u∣≤2M∣x−u∣.|x^2-u^2| \le (|x|+|u|)|x-u| \le 2M|x-u|.∣x2−u2∣≤(∣x∣+∣u∣)∣x−u∣≤2M∣x−u∣.

So f(x)=x2f(x)=x^2f(x)=x2 is Lipschitz on [a,b][a,b][a,b] with constant k=2max⁡{∣a∣,∣b∣}k = 2\max\{|a|,|b|\}k=2max{∣a∣,∣b∣}, and therefore uniformly continuous there — consistent with the Uniform Continuity Theorem, which already guaranteed this since [a,b][a,b][a,b] is closed and bounded and fff is continuous.
